insights: Expense processing time increases monotonically with cost bracket: low-cost expenses take 3.90 days on average, medium-cost 10.53 days, and high-cost 24.24 days — a 522% increase from low to high, explaining 84.31% of variance in processing time.

Category significantly moderates the cost–processing time relationship (p = 4.82e-08), with Assets showing the steepest sensitivity (19.01-day slope) and Miscellaneous the flattest (14.91-day slope), indicating workflow design or approval complexity varies meaningfully by expense type.

Department does not significantly moderate the relationship (p = 0.742), but Product Management exhibits a notable equity concern: its single high-cost expense took 33.0 days — 8.76 days above the overall high-bracket average — suggesting either outlier behavior or systemic risk in high-value approvals for this department.

Customer Support accounts for half (176/344) of all processed expenses and shows the largest volume-weighted slope (20.68 days), making it the dominant driver of the overall cost–delay relationship and a critical focus for efficiency interventions.

Services category has no high-cost completed expenses (n=0), revealing a potential approval bottleneck, policy restriction, or data gap that suppresses observable high-cost processing behavior and limits generalizability of the cost–time relationship for this category.

Low-cost expenses in Product Management (n=2) are processed in 2.5 days on average — 1.4 days faster than the overall low-bracket average — highlighting an inverse equity pattern where low-cost cases are expedited while high-cost ones face extreme delay, pointing to inconsistent application of review rigor.
summary: The analysis confirms a strong, monotonic relationship between expense amount and processing time — high-cost expenses take over six times longer than low-cost ones, with cost bracket alone explaining 84.3% of processing time variance. This is not uniform across categories: Assets show the sharpest escalation, while Miscellaneous is relatively stable; Services lacks any high-cost completions, signaling a possible procedural barrier. Department-level variation is statistically insignificant overall, yet Product Management presents a critical equity anomaly — its only high-cost expense was delayed by nearly 9 days beyond the high-bracket norm, while its low-cost cases were unusually fast. Customer Support drives the bulk of the observed pattern due to volume and steep slope. To improve both efficiency and equity, priority should be placed on reviewing high-cost approval workflows in Assets and Product Management, investigating why Services has no high-cost completions, and validating whether Product Management’s single high-cost case reflects a systemic issue or outlier requiring root-cause follow-up.
